name: Brute Force: Password Spraying
description: Adversaries use a single common password against many accounts (or a small set of passwords against many accounts) to avoid account lockouts. Password spraying is distinct from traditional brute force in that it distributes attempts across many accounts rather than hammering one account. In Azure AD / Entra ID environments this typically appears as a wave of ResultType 50126 (invalid credentials) or 50053 (account locked) events spread across many UserPrincipalName values from one or a small cluster of source IPs.
iocs: Many distinct UserPrincipalName values with ResultType 50126 from a single IPAddress
Attempts spread within a short time window (minutes) to avoid per-account lockout
Low attempt count per account (1–3) but high total volume across accounts
Use of legacy authentication clients: BasicAuthConnector, SMTP, IMAP, POP3
Uniform UserAgent across all failed attempts
ClientAppUsed set to 'Other clients' or legacy protocol names
Attempts targeting high-value accounts: admin@, finance@, hr@, ceo@
Single successful ResultType 0 following a spray pattern from same IP
evasion_variants: Distribute attempts from multiple source IPs in the same /24 subnet
Slow the spray rate to 1 attempt per account per 10 minutes
Rotate UserAgent strings to mimic different browser/OS combinations
Mix in legitimate-looking failed logins (wrong MFA, not invalid password)
Use ResultType 50158 (external security challenge) to blend with MFA friction
Target only accounts that have not had recent successful logins
Attempt logins during business hours to blend with normal traffic
Use OAuth device code flow (ClientAppUsed: 'Microsoft Authentication Broker')
Spread across multiple AppDisplayName values to avoid single-app thresholds
detection_kql_hints: Summarize count() by IPAddress and filter where distinct UserPrincipalName count is high
Look for ResultType in (50126, 50053, 50055) as primary spray indicators
Threshold: same IP, >5 distinct users failing within a 10-minute bin
Flag ClientAppUsed == 'Other clients' combined with failed ResultType
Correlate: IP that caused failures later succeeds (ResultType 0) — likely successful spray
Use dcount(UserPrincipalName) by IPAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 10m) to find spray waves
AuthenticationRequirement == 'singleFactorAuthentication' on legacy clients is a strong signal
